However, surface electromyography (EMG) remains difficult to deploy robustly outside laboratory settings due to electrode sensitivity, signal non-stationarity, and strong subject dependence. In this work, we propose an adaptive IMU-to-EMG learning framework that reconstructs continuous muscle activation envelopes from wearable inertial measurements across heterogeneous movement conditions. The approach combines a Transformer encoder with Gaussian Error Gated Linear Units (GEGLU-Transformer) to enhance cross-subject generalization and enable rapid subject-specific personalization. Under a strict leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) protocol on a multi-condition lower-limb biomechanics dataset, the proposed architecture achieves r = 0.706 +/- 0.139 and R^2 = 0.474 +/- 0.208 without subject-specific adaptation. With only 0.5% adaptation data, performance increases to r = 0.761 +/- 0.030 and R^2 = 0.559 +/- 0.047, demonstrating rapid adaptation and early performance saturation. These results support attention-based architectures combined with lightweight adaptation as a practical and scalable alternative to direct EMG sensing for real-world wearable robotic applications.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a machine learning framework using inertial measurements to estimate muscle activation envelopes, overcoming challenges with using surface EMG in real-world settings.
AI.motivation: Surface EMG is difficult to use reliably outside of controlled environments due to sensitivity issues and variability across subjects, prompting a need for alternative approaches.
AI.method: An adaptive framework using a GEGLU-Transformer encoder is designed to infer muscle activation from inertial sensors, with a focus on cross-subject generalization and rapid subject customization.
AI.result: The model achieves high correlation (r=0.706) and reasonable R^2 values (0.474) under a leave-one-subject-out protocol, and adapts quickly with minimal subject-specific data, improving performance further.
AI.conclusion: The proposed approach offers a scalable and practical solution to estimate neuromuscular activation for wearable robotics, reducing dependence on traditional EMG sensing.

title: Can Code Evaluation Metrics Detect Code Plagiarism?
comment: 10 pages, 5 figures, accepted at LEARNER 2026 workshop (associated with EASE 2026)
summary:
Source Code Plagiarism Detection (SCPD) plays an important role in maintaining fairness and academic integrity in software engineering education. Code Evaluation Metrics (CEMs) are developed for assessing code generation tasks. However, it remains unclear whether such metrics can reliably detect plagiarism across different levels of modification (L1-L6), increasing in complexity.
  In this paper, we perform a comparative empirical study using two open-source labelled datasets, ConPlag (raw and template-free versions) and IRPlag. We evaluate five CEMs, namely CodeBLEU, CrystalBLEU, RUBY, Tree Structured Edit Distance (TSED), and CodeBERTScore. The performance is evaluated using threshold-free ranking-based measures to assess overall, per dataset, and per-level plagiarism performance. The results are compared against state-of-the-art (SOTA) Source Code Plagiarism Detection Tools (SCPDTs), JPlag and Dolos.
  Our findings show that without preprocessing, Dolos achieves the highest overall ranking performance, while among the individual metrics, CrystalBLEU, CodeBLEU, and RUBY outperform JPlag. Performance is strongest at L1 and drops from L4 onward, while CrystalBLEU remains competitive on L6. With preprocessing, CrystalBLEU surpasses Dolos overall. Per dataset, Dolos achieved the best ranking on the ConPlag raw dataset, while CrystalBLEU was the best-performing metric on the remaining datasets. At the plagiarism levels, Dolos remains strongest on L4, while Crystal-BLEU leads most of the remaining difficult levels. These results indicate that CEMs are comparable to dedicated tools in terms of ranking metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper assesses the effectiveness of Code Evaluation Metrics (CEMs) in detecting source code plagiarism across different modification levels, comparing them against established tools.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to evaluate if CEMs can reliably detect plagiarism across various modification complexities in source code, addressing fairness and academic integrity in education.
AI.method: A comparative empirical study is performed using two labeled datasets, ConPlag and IRPlag, evaluating five CEMs with ranking-based measures and comparing results against SOTA tools like JPlag and Dolos.
AI.result: CrystalBLEU, CodeBLEU, and RUBY outperform JPlag, but without preprocessing, Dolos performs best overall. Preprocessing enhances CrystalBLEU’s overall ranking, surpassing Dolos.
AI.conclusion: CEMs can rival dedicated plagiarism detection tools under certain conditions, with CrystalBLEU showing promise even at high complexity levels, especially with preprocessing.

title: Plausible but Wrong: A case study on Agentic Failures in Astrophysical Workflows
summary: Agentic AI systems are increasingly being integrated into scientific workflows, yet their behavior under realistic conditions remains insufficiently understood. We evaluate CMBAgent across two workflow paradigms and eighteen astrophysical tasks. In the One-Shot setting, access to domain-specific context yields an approximately ~6x performance improvement (0.85 vs. ~0 without context), with the primary failure mode being silent incorrect computation - syntactically valid code that produces plausible but inaccurate results. In the Deep Research setting, the system frequently exhibits silent failures across stress tests, producing physically inconsistent posteriors without self-diagnosis. Overall, performance is strong on well-specified tasks but degrades on problems designed to probe reasoning limits, often without visible error signals. These findings highlight that the most concerning failure mode in agentic scientific workflows is not overt failure, but confident generation of incorrect results. We release our evaluation framework to facilitate systematic reliability analysis of scientific AI agents.

AI.tldr: The study evaluates CMBAgent—an AI for astrophysical workflows—under different conditions, finding it prone to silent failures that generate confident but incorrect results.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to better understand and evaluate how agentic AI systems, like CMBAgent, perform in scientific workflows, especially under realistic and challenging conditions.
AI.method: The study assessed CMBAgent's behavior across two workflows: One-Shot (with domain-specific context) and Deep Research (stress tests), covering 18 astrophysical tasks.
AI.result: CMBAgent showed strong performance on straightforward tasks in the One-Shot setting (~6x improvement with context) but struggled in Deep Research, producing physically inconsistent results without self-diagnosis.
AI.conclusion: The paper underscores the critical risk of silent, plausible but inaccurate outputs from AI agents in scientific workflows and calls for systematic evaluation to mitigate these issues.

title: Magnification-Invariant Image Classification via Domain Generalization and Stable Sparse Embedding Signatures
summary: Magnification shift is a major obstacle to robust histopathology classification, because models trained on one imaging scale often generalize poorly to another. Here, we evaluated this problem on the BreaKHis dataset using a strict patient-disjoint leave-one-magnification-out protocol, comparing supervised baseline, baseline augmented with DCGAN-generated patches, and a gradient-reversal domain-general model designed to preserve discriminative information while suppressing magnification-specific variation. Across held-out magnifications, the domain-general model achieved the strongest overall discrimination and its clearest gain was observed when 200X was held out. By contrast, GAN augmentation produced inconsistent effects, improving some folds but degrading others, particularly at 400X. The domain-general model also yielded the lowest Brier score at 0.063 vs 0.089 at baseline. Sparse embedding analysis further revealed that domain-general training reduced average signature size more than three-fold (306 versus 1,074 dimensions) while preserving equivalent predictive performance (AUC: 0.967 vs 0.965; F1: 0.930 vs 0.931). It also increased cross-fold signature reproducibility from near-zero Jaccard overlap in the baseline to 0.99 between the 100X and 200X folds. These findings show that calibrated, compact, and transferable representations can be learned without added architectural complexity, with clear implications for the reliable deployment of computational pathology models across heterogeneous acquisition settings.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses magnification shift in histopathology classification using a domain-general model to improve cross-magnification generalization, outperforming other methods like GAN augmentation.
AI.motivation: Magnification shifts in histopathology hinder the generalization of machine learning models across imaging scales, impacting their reliability in real-world applications.
AI.method: The study employed a patient-disjoint, leave-one-magnification-out evaluation on the BreaKHis dataset, comparing baseline models, GAN-augmented approaches, and a domain-general model with gradient-reversal to mitigate variation due to magnification.
AI.result: The domain-general model achieved superior metrics, including lowest Brier score (0.063), high AUC (0.967), and improved cross-magnification reproducibility, while also reducing feature dimensions and surpassing GAN-augmented methods.
AI.conclusion: A domain-general training approach effectively mitigates magnification-specific artifacts, produces compact and transferable representations, and facilitates robust histopathology classification across heterogeneous image acquisition settings.

title: FAMA: Failure-Aware Meta-Agentic Framework for Open-Source LLMs in Interactive Tool Use Environments
summary: Large Language Models are being increasingly deployed as the decision-making core of autonomous agents capable of effecting change in external environments. Yet, in conversational benchmarks, which simulate real-world customer-centric issue resolution scenarios, these agents frequently fail due to the cascading effects of incorrect decision-making. These challenges are particularly pronounced for open-source LLMs with smaller parameter sizes, limited context windows, and constrained inference budgets, which contribute to increased error accumulation in agentic settings. To tackle these challenges, we present the Failure-Aware Meta-Agentic (FAMA) framework. FAMA operates in two stages: first, it analyzes failure trajectories from baseline agents to identify the most prevalent errors; second, it employs an orchestration mechanism that activates a minimal subset of specialized agents tailored to address these failures by injecting a targeted context for the tool-use agent before the decision-making step. Experiments across open-source LLMs demonstrate performance gains up to 27% across evaluation modes over standard baselines. These results highlight that targeted curation of context through specialized agents to address common failures is a valuable design principle for building reliable, multi-turn tool-use LLM agents that simulate real-world conversational scenarios.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FAMA, a framework designed to improve the performance of LLM-based autonomous agents by analyzing failure patterns and using specialized agents to prevent errors, achieving up to 27% performance gains.
AI.motivation: Large Language Models (LLMs) often struggle in real-world conversational benchmarks, especially open-source models with resource constraints, due to cascading errors in decision-making.
AI.method: The Failure-Aware Meta-Agentic (FAMA) framework operates in two steps: 1) analyzing failure trajectories from baseline agents to identify common errors, and 2) using specialized agents to inject targeted context for improved tool-use and decision-making.
AI.result: Experiments with open-source LLMs show that FAMA improves performance by up to 27% over standard approaches in multi-turn conversational scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Targeted error mitigation through the use of specialized agents is an effective strategy for building reliable LLM-based conversational agents, particularly for multi-turn, tool-use applications.

title: Lightweight Real-Time Rendering Parameter Optimization via XGBoost-Driven Lookup Tables
summary: Achieving a desirable balance between rendering quality and real-time performance is a long-standing challenge in modern game and rendering engines, particularly on resource-constrained mobile devices such as laptops, tablets, and smartphones. Existing approaches to automatic rendering parameter optimization either depend on exhaustive per-scene pre-computation that spans several days, suffer from the prohibitive inference overhead of neural networks that prevents per-frame adaptation, or lack generalizability across heterogeneous hardware and diverse scenes. In this paper, we propose \textbf{LUT-Opt}, a lightweight, general-purpose framework for adaptive per-frame rendering parameter optimization. Our method decomposes the joint optimization of rendering time and image quality into a tractable two-stage pipeline. In the offline stage, we train a pair of XGBoost regressors to predict rendering time and image quality from rendering parameters, hardware state, and scene complexity descriptors. The trained ensemble models are then distilled into compact lookup tables (LUTs) through systematic discretization and a two-phase linear search that first constrains rendering time and subsequently maximizes structural similarity (SSIM). During runtime, the pre-computed LUT is queried every frame in sub-millisecond time, enabling truly adaptive parameter selection with negligible computational overhead. We validate LUT-Opt on two representative rendering techniques -- subsurface scattering (SSS) and hybrid-pipeline ambient occlusion (AO) -- implemented within Unreal Engine 5. Extensive experiments across multiple scenes and GPU configurations demonstrate that LUT-Opt reduces subsurface scattering rendering time by approximately 40\% and ambient occlusion rendering time by roughly 70\%, while incurring only about 2\% increase in image quality error, with per-frame inference latency below 0.1\ ms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LUT-Opt, a framework for real-time rendering parameter optimization with low computational overhead.
AI.motivation: The study is driven by the difficulties in balancing high-quality rendering and real-time performance on resource-constrained devices, and limitations in existing optimization approaches.
AI.method: LUT-Opt employs a two-stage approach: training XGBoost regressors offline for rendering prediction and distilling results into compact LUTs, which are queried during runtime for efficient parameter optimization.
AI.result: The framework reduced rendering time by 40% for subsurface scattering (SSS) and 70% for ambient occlusion (AO), with minimal quality degradation and sub-millisecond inference latency.
AI.conclusion: LUT-Opt achieves efficient and adaptive per-frame rendering parameter optimization, demonstrating its practicality for real-time applications on diverse hardware configurations.

title: Generative diffusion models for spatiotemporal influenza forecasting
summary: Forecasting infectious disease incidence can provide important information to guide public health planning, yet is difficult because epidemic dynamics are complex. Current mechanistic and statistical approaches often struggle to capture multimodal uncertainty or emergent trends. Influpaint adapts denoising diffusion probabilistic models to epidemic forecasting. By encoding influenza seasons as spatiotemporal images in which pixel intensity represents incidence, Influpaint learns a rich distribution of disease dynamics from a hybrid dataset of surveillance and simulated trajectories. Forecasting is formulated as a conditional generation (inpainting) task from partial observations. We show that Influpaint generates realistic, diverse epidemic trajectories and achieves forecast accuracy that is competitive with leading ensemble methods in retrospective evaluation. In real-time evaluation during the 2023--2025 U.S. CDC FluSight challenges, performance improved substantially across seasons, with highly accurate but somewhat overconfident projections in 2024--2025. The best performance was achieved with a training dataset containing 30% surveillance and 70% simulated trajectories. These results show that diffusion models can capture important spatiotemporal structure in influenza dynamics and provide a flexible framework for probabilistic infectious disease forecasting.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Influpaint, a forecasting model for infectious diseases, based on denoising diffusion probabilistic models, and applied it to influenza forecasting to achieve competitive accuracy.
AI.motivation: Forecasting infectious diseases is crucial for public health but remains challenging due to complex and uncertain epidemic dynamics.
AI.method: The authors adapt denoising diffusion probabilistic models for forecasting by encoding disease patterns as spatiotemporal images and training on a mix of real-world and simulated datasets.
AI.result: Influpaint performed competitively in retrospective evaluations and real-time CDC FluSight challenges, with its performance improving over seasons, and the best accuracy achieved with a 30% surveillance and 70% simulated data mix.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion models like Influpaint offer a promising and adaptable framework for probabilistic forecasting by capturing detailed spatiotemporal structures in infectious disease dynamics.

title: Learning-Based Dynamics Modeling and Robust Control for Tendon-Driven Continuum Robots
summary: Tendon-Driven Continuum Robots (TDCRs) pose significant modeling and control challenges due to complex nonlinearities, such as frictional hysteresis and transmission compliance. This paper proposes a differentiable learning framework that integrates high-fidelity dynamics modeling with robust neural control. We develop a GRU-based dynamics model featuring bidirectional multi-channel connectivity and residual prediction to effectively suppress compounding errors during long-horizon auto-regressive prediction. By treating this model as a gradient bridge, an end-to-end neural control policy is optimized through backpropagation, allowing it to implicitly internalize compensation for intricate nonlinearities. Experimental validation on a physical three-section TDCR demonstrates that our framework achieves accurate tracking and superior robustness against unseen payloads, outperforming Jacobian-based methods by eliminating self-excited oscillations.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a learning framework for Tendon-Driven Continuum Robots using a GRU-based model and neural control to tackle nonlinear dynamics challenges, achieving robust experimental results.
AI.motivation: Modeling and controlling TDCRs are difficult due to their nonlinear behaviors such as frictional hysteresis and transmission compliance, which pose obstacles to achieving accurate tracking and robustness.
AI.method: The framework uses a GRU-based dynamics model with bidirectional multi-channel connectivity and residual prediction, enabling long-horizon prediction accuracy. This model acts as a gradient bridge for optimizing end-to-end neural control policies.
AI.result: In experiments on a physical three-section TDCR, the framework demonstrated improved tracking accuracy and robustness against unseen payloads, outperforming traditional Jacobian-based methods.
AI.conclusion: By integrating high-fidelity modeling and neural control, this approach effectively compensates for nonlinearities, showcasing superior tracking performance and robust control while avoiding self-excited oscillations.

title: Key Developer Roles and Organizational Coupling in Microservices: A Longitudinal Analysis
summary: Microservice-based systems impose significant organizational coordination challenges, yet the role of individual developers in shaping organizational coupling (OC) remains underexplored. Prior work largely focuses on structural architectural aspects, leaving gaps in understanding how developer roles influence coordination dynamics over time. This study investigates how different developer roles contribute to OC in a large-scale microservices system. The analysis focuses on three key roles, namely Jacks, representing broad knowledge holders, Mavens, representing deep specialists, and Connectors, representing organizational bridges. A longitudinal repository mining analysis of GitHub data, including commits and issue and pull request interactions, is conducted to operationalize OC and quantify its evolution over time. The results show that Connectors are consistently associated with higher levels of OC, while the co-occurrence of multiple roles within the same developer further amplifies coupling effects. In contrast, Jacks and Mavens exhibit more localized and role-specific influences. These findings indicate that OC in microservices is primarily a role-driven phenomenon rather than an inevitable structural property, providing a foundation for role-aware organizational design and targeted decoupling strategies.

AI.tldr: This paper examines how different developer roles impact organizational coupling (OC) in microservice-based systems, identifying roles like Jacks, Mavens, and Connectors and analyzing their influence using GitHub data.
AI.motivation: To investigate the underexplored role of individual developer roles in shaping organizational coupling in microservices, beyond structural architectural factors.
AI.method: A longitudinal repository mining analysis of GitHub data, focusing on commits, issues, and pull requests, to study the impact of three key developer roles (Jacks, Mavens, Connectors) on OC.
AI.result: Connectors are associated with higher OC, and combining multiple roles in one developer further amplifies coupling effects, while Jacks and Mavens have more localized impacts.
AI.conclusion: OC in microservice systems is driven primarily by developer roles, not structural properties, suggesting role-aware organizational design can optimize coordination.

title: Multi-action Tangled Program Graphs for Multi-task Reinforcement Learning with Continuous Control
summary: Over the past few decades, machine learning has been widely used to learn complex tasks. Reinforcement Learning (RL), inspired by human behavior, is a great example, as it involves developing specific behaviours for specific tasks. To further challenge algorithms, Multi-Task RL (MTRL) environments have been introduced, requiring a single model to learn multiple behaviors. The Tangled Program Graph (TPG) algorithm is a Genetic Programming (GP) algorithm designed for discrete MTRL environments. Recently, the MAPLE algorithm has been proposed, as another GP algorithm that achieves high results in single task continuous RL environments. A variation of the TPG is proposed alongside MAPLE, named Multi-Action TPG (MATPG) that aggregates MAPLE agents, and creates a control flow to activate them. Initially tested on single task RL environments only, MATPG achieved similar results to MAPLE. In this work, we present a new benchmark based on the MuJoCo Half Cheetah from Gymnasium. This benchmark features five distinct obstacles that are randomly positioned in front of the agent, each of which demands a unique behavior. This benchmark serves as a use case for MATPG, to prove its ability as a GP solution for continuous MTRL environments. Our experiments demonstrate its superiority in this multi-task use case when combined with lexicase selection. Furthermore, we examine the interpretability of the evolved graph, revealing that the decision flow of the model is fully interpretable.

AI.tldr: This paper focuses on a Genetic Programming algorithm, Multi-Action TPG (MATPG), aimed at tackling multi-task reinforcement learning (MTRL) environments with a new benchmark using MuJoCo Half Cheetah.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to advance solutions in continuous multi-task reinforcement learning (MTRL) by developing methods that combine high performance in task diversity with interpretability, especially using genetic programming.
AI.method: A modified Tangled Program Graph algorithm named Multi-Action TPG (MATPG) was tested on a newly defined benchmark with five distinct tasks featuring obstacles for the MuJoCo Half Cheetah environment. The algorithm employs lexicase selection and incorporates interpretability analysis of its evolved decision graph.
AI.result: MATPG demonstrated superior performance in multi-task reinforcement learning scenarios compared to existing approaches. Additionally, its decision flow was fully interpretable.
AI.conclusion: MATPG proves to be an effective Genetic Programming solution for continuous MTRL settings, showcasing both high performance and interpretability, validated on a novel benchmark designed for multi-task challenges.

title: Model-agnostic information transfer and fusion for classification with label noise
summary: Label noise presents a fundamental challenge in modern machine learning, especially when large-scale datasets are generated via automated processes. An increasingly common and important data paradigm, particularly in domains like medical imaging, involves learning from a large dataset with coarse, noisy labels supplemented by a small, expert-verified, clean dataset. This setting constitutes a typical information transfer and fusion problem. However, the significant distribution shift between the noisy and clean data violates the core overall parametric similarity assumptions of existing statistical transfer learning methods, while their reliance on parametric models is ill-suited for complex data like images. To address these limitations, this paper develops a generic model-agnostic nonparametric framework for classification with label noise, which applies to a broad class of classifiers. Our approach leverages the small clean dataset to ``purify'' the large noisy one and carefully manages the remaining ambiguous samples. This framework is underpinned by a rigorous statistical theory. Its empirical performance is demonstrated through simulations and a real-world application to medical image analysis for pneumonia diagnosis.

AI.tldr: Addressing label noise in learning by purifying noisy datasets using clean data in a model-agnostic nonparametric framework.
AI.motivation: The growing need to manage label noise in large datasets, particularly when supplemented by smaller, expert-verified, clean datasets, highlights a gap in current methods due to their parametric assumptions and limitations for complex data like images.
AI.method: A model-agnostic nonparametric framework is introduced, leveraging small clean datasets to purify noisy ones and handling ambiguous samples, supported by statistical theory.
AI.result: The proposed approach shows efficacy in simulations and a real-world scenario involving pneumonia diagnosis in medical imaging.
AI.conclusion: This framework offers a robust solution for improving classification under label noise conditions, especially for complex, noisy datasets and diverse classifiers.

title: Frictive Policy Optimization for LLMs: Epistemic Intervention, Risk-Sensitive Control, and Reflective Alignment
comment: Frictive Policy Optimization; epistemic alignment; risk-sensitive control; LLM alignment; clarification and refusal; preference learning; trust regions; dialogue agents
summary: We propose Frictive Policy Optimization (FPO), a framework for learning language model policies that regulate not only what to say, but when and how to intervene in order to manage epistemic and normative risk. Unlike standard alignment methods that optimize surface-level preference or task utility, FPO treats clarification, verification, challenge, redirection, and refusal as explicit control actions whose purpose is to shape the evolution of belief, commitment, and uncertainty over time. We formalize alignment as a risk-sensitive epistemic control problem in which intervention decisions are selected based on their expected effect on downstream epistemic quality rather than on immediate reward alone. We introduce a compact taxonomy of frictive interventions, a structured friction functional that operationalizes multiple alignment failure modes, and a unified family of FPO methods spanning reward shaping, preference pairing, group-relative ranking, and risk-conditioned trust regions. We further propose an evaluation framework that measures epistemic competence directly through clarification behavior, calibration, contradiction repair, refusal proportionality, and information efficiency. Together, these results provide a formal and algorithmic foundation for learning agents that are aligned not only in outcome, but in epistemic conduct.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Frictive Policy Optimization (FPO), a framework for optimizing language models to not only generate responses but manage risks by deciding when and how to intervene during interactions.
AI.motivation: Standard alignment methods inadequately address the dynamics of belief and uncertainty management. The authors aim to build systems that better handle epistemic and normative risks over time.
AI.method: The authors formalize alignment as an epistemic control problem, propose a taxonomy of intervention types, and introduce FPO methods that optimize interventions based on downstream epistemic quality instead of immediate outcomes.
AI.result: They outline an evaluation framework for epistemic competence, including metrics like clarification behavior and refusal proportionality, and define methods for improving epistemic alignment.
AI.conclusion: The paper lays down a formal and algorithmic foundation for building language models aligned not just on outcomes but in managing belief and uncertainty during interactions.

title: FCMBench-Video: Benchmarking Document Video Intelligence
summary: Document understanding is a critical capability in financial credit review, onboarding, and remote verification, where both decision accuracy and evidence traceability matter. Compared with static document images, document videos present a temporally redundant and sequentially unfolding evidence stream, require evidence integration across frames, and preserve acquisition-process cues relevant to authenticity-sensitive and anti-fraud review. We introduce FCMBench-Video, a benchmark for document-video intelligence that evaluates document perception, temporal grounding, and evidence-grounded reasoning under realistic capture conditions. For privacy-compliant yet realistic data at scale, we organize construction as an atomic-acquisition and composition workflow that records reusable single-document clips, applies controlled degradations, and assembles long-form multi-document videos with prescribed temporal spans. FCMBench-Video is built from 495 atomic videos composed into 1,200 long-form videos paired with 11,322 expert-annotated question--answer instances, covering 28 document types over 20s--60s duration tiers and 5,960 Chinese / 5,362 English instances. Evaluations on nine recent Video-MLLMs show that FCMBench-Video provides meaningful separation across systems and capabilities: counting is the most duration-sensitive task, Cross-Document Validation and Evidence-Grounded Selection probe higher-level evidence integration, and Visual Prompt Injection provides a complementary robustness dimension. The overall score distribution is broad and approximately bell-shaped, indicating a benchmark that is neither saturated nor dominated by trivial cases. Together, these results position FCMBench-Video as a reproducible benchmark for tracking Video-MLLM progress on document-video understanding and probing capability boundaries in authenticity-sensitive credit-domain applications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces FCMBench-Video, a benchmark focusing on understanding document-videos for financial credit applications, highlighting its evaluation of decision-making under realistic, anti-fraud conditions.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of document analysis in financial credit processes where video evidence is critical for decision accuracy and fraud detection.
AI.method: The authors created FCMBench-Video by leveraging atomic video acquisition to simulate real-world conditions, constructing a large-scale dataset, and evaluating recent Video-MLLM models on diverse document types and tasks.
AI.result: FCMBench-Video demonstrates effective differentiation across models and tasks, showing its utility in analyzing decision accuracy, task complexity, and robustness.
AI.conclusion: The benchmark establishes itself as a reliable tool for advancing document-video intelligence in fraud-sensitive domains, offering scope for future model improvements.

title: Reference-Augmented Learning for Precise Tracking Policy of Tendon-Driven Continuum Robots
summary: Tendon-Driven Continuum Robots (TDCRs) pose significant control challenges due to their highly nonlinear, path-dependent dynamics and non-Markovian characteristics. Traditional Jacobian-based controllers often struggle with hysteresis-induced oscillations, while conventional learning-based approaches suffer from poor generalization to out-of-distribution trajectories. This paper proposes a reference-augmented offline learning framework for precise 6-DOF tracking control of TDCRs. By leveraging a differentiable RNN-based dynamics surrogate as a gradient bridge, we optimize a control policy through an augmented reference distribution. This multi-scale augmentation scheme incorporates stochastic bias, harmonic perturbations, and random walks, forcing the policy to internalize diverse tracking error recovery mechanisms without additional hardware interaction. Experimental results on a three-section TDCR platform demonstrate that the proposed policy achieves a 50.9\% reduction in average position error compared to non-augmented baselines and significantly outperforms Jacobian-based methods in both precision and stability across various speeds.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses control challenges in Tendon-Driven Continuum Robots (TDCRs) by proposing a reference-augmented learning framework using a differentiable RNN-based dynamics model, achieving significant improvements in tracking control performance.
AI.motivation: The paper is driven by the need to overcome the control difficulties in TDCRs due to their nonlinear and non-Markovian behavior, as traditional methods and conventional learning strategies perform poorly in these scenarios.
AI.method: An offline learning framework is designed, leveraging a differentiable RNN-based dynamics surrogate for gradient optimization, alongside an augmented reference distribution that includes multi-scale perturbations to improve control policy learning.
AI.result: Experiments on a three-section TDCR platform showed a 50.9% reduction in average position error compared to baselines and better precision and stability than Jacobian-based methods at various speeds.
AI.conclusion: The proposed reference-augmented framework enhances the precision and stability of TDCR control systems, overcoming the limitations of traditional and common learning-based approaches.

title: Does social identity matter in software engineering? Assessing the case of research software engineers
comment: 12 pages, 2 figures. International Conference on Evaluation and Assessment in Software Engineering. June 9-12, 2026, Glasgow, United Kingdom
summary: Social identity is a concept from psychology that refers to the part of an individual's identity that derives from their group membership(s). In this paper, we explore social identity in members of the professional community of Research Software Engineers (RSEs). Using a mixed-methods approach, our study combined computational linguistic analysis and inferential statistics to examine over 28,000 social media posts, 1,700 blogs, and survey responses from 381 professional RSEs. The findings highlight the emergence of a collective RSE identity and demonstrate its role in shaping professional wellbeing. This study contributes an interdisciplinary perspective by integrating social psychology and software engineering to show how a professional identity evolves and why it matters.

AI.tldr: This paper investigates the social identity of Research Software Engineers (RSEs) using computational analysis and surveys, finding a collective identity and its impact on professional wellbeing.
AI.motivation: To understand how a collective professional identity forms among Research Software Engineers and its significance for their wellbeing.
AI.method: A mixed-methods approach involving computational linguistic analysis, inferential statistics, and examination of social media posts, blogs, and survey responses of RSEs.
AI.result: The findings show the emergence of a collective identity amongst RSEs and its influence on their professional wellbeing.
AI.conclusion: The study bridges social psychology and software engineering fields to highlight the significance of collective identity in the professional domain and its implications for wellbeing.

title: JURY-RL: Votes Propose, Proofs Dispose for Label-Free RLVR
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) enhances the reasoning of large language models (LLMs), but standard RLVR often depends on human-annotated answers or carefully curated reward specifications. In machine-checkable domains, label-free alternatives such as majority voting or LLM-as-a-judge remove annotation cost but can introduce false positives that destabilize training. We introduce JURY-RL, a label-free RLVR framework that decouples answer proposal from reward disposal: votes from model rollouts propose a candidate answer, and a formal verifier determines whether that candidate can receive positive reward. Concretely, only rollouts matching the plurality-voted answer are rewarded when that answer is successfully verified in Lean. When verification is inconclusive, we invoke ResZero (Residual-Zero), a fallback reward that discards the unverified plurality proposal and redistributes a zero-mean, variance-preserving signal over the residual answers. This design maintains a stable optimization gradient without reinforcing unverifiable consensus. Across three backbone models trained on mathematical data, JURY-RL consistently outperforms other label-free baselines on mathematical reasoning benchmarks and transfers competitively to code generation and general benchmarks. It attains pass@1 performance comparable to supervised ground-truth training, with superior generalization demonstrated by higher pass@k and response diversity.

AI.tldr: JURY-RL is a label-free reinforcement learning framework that enhances reasoning in large language models by using formal verification and a fallback mechanism to stabilize training in machine-checkable domains. It outperforms baselines, achieving competitive generalization and performance.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the challenge of using reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) in large language models, especially without reliance on human annotations or curated rewards, which are costly and error-prone.
AI.method: The proposed method, JURY-RL, decouples answer proposal and reward disposal. It uses model rollouts for generating candidate answers, verified by a formal verifier. Additionally, it uses a fallback mechanism, ResZero, to prevent reinforcing unverifiable results while maintaining stable optimization gradients.
AI.result: JURY-RL surpasses other label-free baselines in mathematical reasoning tasks and transfers well to code generation and general benchmarks. It achieves performance comparable to supervised learning with ground-truth annotations and shows better generalization and diversity.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that JURY-RL is a robust and effective label-free framework for RLVR. It not only reduces annotation costs but also achieves superior performance and generalization in reasoning tasks for mathematical and other domains.

title: When Errors Can Be Beneficial: A Categorization of Imperfect Rewards for Policy Gradient
summary: Training language models via reinforcement learning often relies on imperfect proxy rewards, since ground truth rewards that precisely define the intended behavior are rarely available. Standard metrics for assessing the quality of proxy rewards, such as ranking accuracy, treat incorrect rewards as strictly harmful. In this work, however, we highlight that not all deviations from the ground truth are equal. By theoretically analyzing which outputs attract probability during policy gradient optimization, we categorize reward errors according to their effect on the increase in ground truth reward. The analysis establishes that reward errors, though conventionally viewed as harmful, can also be benign or even beneficial by preventing the policy from stalling around outputs with mediocre ground truth reward. We then present two practical implications of our theory. First, for reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF), we develop reward model evaluation metrics that account for the harmfulness of reward errors. Compared to standard ranking accuracy, these metrics typically correlate better with the performance of a language model after RLHF, yet gaps remain in robustly evaluating reward models. Second, we provide insights for reward design in settings with verifiable rewards. A key theme underlying our results is that the effectiveness of a proxy reward function depends heavily on its interaction with the initial policy and learning algorithm.

AI.tldr: The paper explores how not all reward errors during reinforcement learning are equally harmful, and introduces methods to assess and design reward functions effectively.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the issue of imperfect proxy rewards during reinforcement learning, as ground truth rewards are rarely available. The authors question the conventional view that all reward deviations are strictly negative.
AI.method: The paper provides a theoretical analysis categorizing reward errors based on their effects on policy gradient optimization. It proposes new metrics for RLHF reward evaluation and insights for reward design in settings with verifiable rewards.
AI.result: The study reveals that some reward errors can be benign or beneficial by preventing suboptimal policy stagnation. The proposed reward evaluation metrics correlate better with post-RLHF model performance and provide insights into reward function design.
AI.conclusion: The paper challenges the traditional viewpoint that all reward errors are harmful and emphasizes that proxy rewards must be designed with consideration of their interaction with the initial policy and learning algorithm.

title: CroSearch-R1: Better Leveraging Cross-lingual Knowledge for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: A multilingual collection may contain useful knowledge in other languages to supplement and correct the facts in the original language for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). However, the vanilla approach that simply concatenates multiple pieces of knowledge from different languages into the context may fail to improve effectiveness due to the potential disparities across languages. To better leverage multilingual knowledge, we propose CroSearch-R1, a search-augmented reinforcement learning framework to integrate multilingual knowledge into the Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) process. In particular, the approach adopts a multi-turn retrieval strategy with cross-lingual knowledge integration to dynamically align the knowledge from other languages as supplementary evidence into a unified representation space. Furthermore, we introduce a multilingual rollout mechanism to optimize reasoning transferability across languages. Experimental results demonstrate that our framework effectively leverages cross-lingual complementarity and improves the effectiveness of RAG with multilingual collections.

AI.tldr: This paper presents CroSearch-R1, a framework to enhance Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) using multilingual knowledge.
AI.motivation: The study aims to address the challenge of effectively utilizing multilingual knowledge to improve RAG, which standard approaches fail to do due to cross-language disparities.
AI.method: The authors propose a framework, CroSearch-R1, which uses reinforcement learning, multi-turn retrieval, knowledge alignment, and a multilingual rollout mechanism to integrate and optimize multilingual knowledge.
AI.result: Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed framework improves the effectiveness of RAG by utilizing cross-lingual complementary knowledge.
AI.conclusion: CroSearch-R1 successfully integrates multilingual knowledge into RAG, overcoming challenges of cross-language disparities and enhancing effectiveness.

title: Image Classification via Random Dilated Convolution with Multi-Branch Feature Extraction and Context Excitation
summary: Image classification remains a fundamental yet challenging task in computer vision, particularly when fine-grained feature extraction and background noise suppression are required simultaneously. Conventional convolutional neural networks, despite their remarkable success in hierarchical feature learning, often struggle with capturing multi-scale contextual information and are susceptible to overfitting when confronted with noisy or irrelevant image regions. In this paper, we propose RDCNet (Image Classification Network with Random Dilated Convolution), a novel architecture built upon ResNet-34 that integrates three synergistic innovations to address these limitations: (1) a Multi-Branch Random Dilated Convolution (MRDC) module that employs parallel branches with varying dilation rates combined with a stochastic masking mechanism to capture fine-grained features across multiple scales while enhancing robustness against noise and overfitting; (2) a Fine-Grained Feature Enhancement (FGFE) module embedded within MRDC that bridges global contextual information with local feature representations through adaptive pooling and bilinear interpolation, thereby amplifying sensitivity to subtle visual patterns; and (3) a Context Excitation (CE) module that leverages softmax-based spatial attention and channel recalibration to dynamically emphasize task-relevant features while suppressing background interference. Extensive experiments conducted on five benchmark datasets -- CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, Imagenette, and Imagewoof -- demonstrate that RDCNet consistently achieves state-of-the-art classification accuracy, outperforming the second-best competing methods by margins of 0.02\%, 1.12\%, 0.18\%, 4.73\%, and 3.56\%, respectively, thereby validating the effectiveness and generalizability of the proposed approach across diverse visual recognition scenarios.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces RDCNet, a ResNet-34-based architecture designed to enhance image classification by addressing challenges like multi-scale feature extraction and noise suppression, outperforming several state-of-the-art methods on diverse datasets.
AI.motivation: The motivation stems from the limitations of conventional CNNs in handling multi-scale contextual information and their vulnerability to noise and overfitting in fine-grained image classification tasks.
AI.method: RDCNet incorporates three key innovations: Multi-Branch Random Dilated Convolution for feature extraction at multiple scales, a Fine-Grained Feature Enhancement module for merging global and local features, and a Context Excitation module for dynamic attention to task-relevant regions.
AI.result: The model achieved state-of-the-art performance across five datasets (CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, SVHN, Imagenette, and Imagewoof), surpassing the next-best methods with significant margins.
AI.conclusion: RDCNet effectively addresses inherent challenges in image classification, proving its robustness and generalizability in diverse scenarios.

title: Rethinking Layer Redundancy in Large Language Models: Calibration Objectives and Search for Depth Pruning
summary: Depth pruning improves the inference efficiency of large language models by removing Transformer blocks. Prior work has focused on importance criteria and search algorithms, often treating layer redundancy as an inherent structural property of pretrained networks. In contrast, we adopt a \emph{functional perspective}, where redundancy is jointly influenced by the model and the evaluation objective, suggesting that a universal ranking may not be sufficient. Through an empirical study across three LLM families, two calibration objectives, and seven search algorithms, we observe that different objectives yield qualitatively different redundant layers, and that perplexity and downstream accuracy rankings do not consistently align. Under a fixed objective, however, search algorithms tend to produce similar solutions. Overall, our results suggest that the calibration objective may play a more influential role than the choice of search algorithm, indicating that further attention to objective design could be beneficial.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates depth pruning in large language models by adopting a functional perspective, showing that redundancy is objective-dependent and emphasizing the importance of calibration objectives.
AI.motivation: To improve the inference efficiency of large language models while recognizing that redundancy is influenced by both model structure and evaluation objectives.
AI.method: Conducted an empirical study across three LLM families, two calibration objectives, and seven search algorithms to observe the relationship between redundancy, objectives, and rankings.
AI.result: Found that redundancy patterns vary with objectives, rankings for perplexity and downstream accuracy diverge, but similar solutions emerge across search algorithms under fixed objectives.
AI.conclusion: Calibration objectives are more impactful than the choice of search algorithm, suggesting focus on objective design to enhance depth pruning techniques.

title: Sensitivity-Based Tube NMPC for Cooperative Aerial Structures Under Parametric Uncertainty
comment: Accepted to the 2026 International Conference on Unmanned Aircraft Systems, ICUAS 2026
summary: This paper presents a sensitivity-based tube Nonlinear Model Predictive Control (NMPC) framework for cooperative aerial chains under bounded parametric uncertainty. We consider a planar two-vehicle chain connected by rigid links, modeled with input-rate actuation to enforce slew-rate and magnitude limits on thrust and torque. Robustness to uncertainty in link mass, length, and inertia is achieved by propagating first-order parametric state sensitivities along the horizon and using them to compute online constraint-tightening margins. We robustify an inter-link separation constraint, implemented via a smooth cosine embedding, and thrust-magnitude bounds. The method is implemented in MATLAB and evaluated with boundary-hugging maneuvers and Monte-Carlo uncertainty sampling. Results show improved constraint margins under uncertainty with tracking performance comparable to nominal NMPC.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a robust sensitivity-based tube NMPC algorithm for aerial vehicle chains to handle parametric uncertainties in link properties.
AI.motivation: To address robustness issues under parametric uncertainties in aerial vehicle chains, specifically focusing on link mass, length, and inertia variations.
AI.method: The authors incorporate first-order parametric state sensitivities into constraint tightening for robustness and validate their NMPC framework using MATLAB with Monte Carlo simulations.
AI.result: The proposed approach improves constraint margins under uncertainties while maintaining tracking performance comparable to standard NMPC.
AI.conclusion: The sensitivity-based tube NMPC framework offers enhanced robustness to parametric uncertainties with minimal trade-off in control performance, making it suitable for cooperative aerial vehicle chains.

title: RESTestBench: A Benchmark for Evaluating the Effectiveness of LLM-Generated REST API Test Cases from NL Requirements
summary: Existing REST API testing tools are typically evaluated using code coverage and crash-based fault metrics. However, recent LLM-based approaches increasingly generate tests from NL requirements to validate functional behaviour, making traditional metrics weak proxies for whether generated tests validate intended behaviour. To address this gap, we present RESTestBench, a benchmark comprising three REST services paired with manually verified NL requirements in both precise and vague variants, enabling controlled and reproducible evaluation of requirement-based test generation. RESTestBench further introduces a requirements-based mutation testing metric that measures the fault-detection effectiveness of a generated test case with respect to a specific requirement, extending the property-based approach of Bartocci et al. . Using RESTestBench, we evaluate two approaches across multiple state-of-the-art LLMs: (i) non-refinement-based generation, and (ii) refinement-based generation guided by interaction with the running SUT. In the refinement experiments, RESTestBench assesses how exposure to the actual implementation, valid or mutated, affects test effectiveness. Our results show that test effectiveness drops considerably when the generator interacts with faulty or mutated code, especially for vague requirements, sometimes negating the benefit of refinement and indicating that incorporating actual SUT behaviour is unnecessary when requirement detail is high.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RESTestBench, a benchmark for evaluating REST API test generation from natural language (NL) requirements. It assesses two approaches and reveals insights into the role of requirement specificity and system behavior.
AI.motivation: Traditional metrics like code coverage and crash-based faults poorly capture the ability of generated tests to validate functional behavior from NL requirements, given the rise of LLM-based test generation approaches.
AI.method: RESTestBench comprises REST services paired with NL requirements in precise and vague forms, along with a mutation testing metric to evaluate fault-detection based on specific requirements. It benchmarks two test generation approaches: non-refinement-based and refinement-based.
AI.result: The evaluation shows significant drops in test effectiveness when the generator interacts with faulty or mutated implementations, particularly with vague requirements. High-detail requirements reduce the need for incorporating actual system behavior.
AI.conclusion: RESTestBench is a valuable tool for studying NL-based REST API test generation, demonstrating that vague requirements and interaction with faulty systems can undermine test effectiveness, while detailed requirements minimize reliance on system interactions.

title: PI-TTA: Physics-Informed Source-Free Test-Time Adaptation for Robust Human Activity Recognition on Mobile Devices
summary: Source-free test-time adaptation (TTA) is appealing for mobile and wearable sensing because it enables on-device personalization from unlabeled test streams without centralizing private data. However, sensor-based human activity recognition (HAR) poses challenges that are less pronounced in standard vision benchmarks: behavioral inertial streams are temporally correlated and often exhibit within-session shifts caused by sensor rotation, placement change, and sampling-rate drift. Under this streaming non-i.i.d. setting, widely used vision-style TTA objectives can become unstable, leading to overconfident errors, representation collapse, and catastrophic forgetting. We propose PI-TTA, a lightweight source-free adaptation framework that stabilizes online updates through three physics-consistent constraints: gravity consistency, short-horizon temporal continuity, and spectral stability. PI-TTA updates the same small parameter subset as strong source-free baselines and incurs only modest overhead, making it suitable for on-device deployment. Experiments on USCHAD, PAMAP2, and mHealth under long-sequence stress tests and factorized shift protocols show that PI-TTA mitigates the severe degradation observed in confidence-driven baselines and preserves stable adaptation under sustained streaming conditions. It improves long-sequence accuracy by up to 9.13% and reduces physical-violation rates by 27.5%, 24.1%, and 45.4% on USCHAD, PAMAP2, and mHealth, respectively. These results demonstrate that physics-informed adaptation can improve accuracy, stability, and deployment reliability for real-world mobile sensing systems.

AI.tldr: PI-TTA is a source-free test-time adaptation framework designed to handle non-i.i.d. streaming challenges in sensor-based human activity recognition, showing significant improvements in accuracy and stability.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in source-free test-time adaptation for wearable sensing, specifically those posed by streaming behavioral inertial data that are non-i.i.d. and impacted by within-session shifts.
AI.method: The method, PI-TTA, introduces three physics-consistent constraints—gravity consistency, short-horizon temporal continuity, and spectral stability—to stabilize online model updates while maintaining lightweight operations for on-device use.
AI.result: PI-TTA demonstrated up to 9.13% improvement in long-sequence accuracy and significant reductions in physical-violation rates across three datasets—USCHAD, PAMAP2, and mHealth—under stress-test conditions.
AI.conclusion: Physics-informed constraints in on-device adaptation frameworks like PI-TTA enhance accuracy, stability, and reliability in real-world mobile sensing applications.

title: Teacher Forcing as Generalized Bayes: Optimization Geometry Mismatch in Switching Surrogates for Chaotic Dynamics
comment: Presented at the Workshop on Optimization and Post-Bayesian Inference in Machine Learning, AISTATS 2026
summary: Identity teacher forcing (ITF) enables stable training of deterministic recurrent surrogates for chaotic dynamical systems and has been highly effective for dynamical systems reconstruction (DSR) with recurrent neural networks (RNNs), including interpretable almost-linear RNNs (AL-RNNs). However, as an intervention-based prediction loss (and thus a generalized Bayes update), teacher forcing need not match the free-running model's marginal likelihood geometry. We compare the objective-induced curvatures of ITF and marginal likelihood in a probabilistic switching augmentation of AL-RNNs, estimating ambiguity-aware observed information via Louis' identity. In the switching setting studied here, conditioning on a single forced regime path (as ITF does) inflates curvature, while marginal likelihood curvature is reduced by a missing-information correction when multiple switching explanations remain plausible. In Lorenz-63 experiments, windowed evidence fine-tuning improves held-out evidence but can degrade dynamical quantities of interest (QoIs) relative to ITF-pretrained models.

AI.tldr: The paper explores identity teacher forcing (ITF) in training recurrent surrogates for chaotic dynamical systems, assessing its alignment with the model's marginal likelihood and demonstrating nuances via Lorenz-63 experiments.
AI.motivation: To address mismatches between ITF and marginal likelihood geometry in training RNNs for chaotic systems and to investigate the impact of probabilistic switching augmentation on model performance.
AI.method: The study employs a probabilistic switching approach to augment AL-RNNs, evaluates objective-induced curvatures of ITF and marginal likelihood using Louis' identity, and compares implications on dynamical system reconstruction via experiments on the Lorenz-63 system.
AI.result: ITF inflates curvature compared to marginal likelihood due to conditioning on a single regime path. Fine-tuning with windowed evidence enhances held-out evidence but may adversely affect certain dynamical quantities of interest.
AI.conclusion: While identity teacher forcing is effective for initial training, its divergence from marginal likelihood geometry suggests potential trade-offs in model performance, especially with probabilistic switching and evidence fine-tuning.

title: BARRED: Synthetic Training of Custom Policy Guardrails via Asymmetric Debate
summary: Deploying guardrails for custom policies remains challenging, as generic safety models fail to capture task-specific requirements, while prompting LLMs suffers from inconsistent boundary-case performance and high inference costs. Training custom classifiers achieves both accuracy and efficiency, yet demands substantial labeled data that is costly to obtain. We present BARRED (Boundary Alignment Refinement through REflection and Debate), a framework for generating faithful and diverse synthetic training data using only a task description and a small set of unlabeled examples. Our approach decomposes the domain space into dimensions to ensure comprehensive coverage, and employs multi-agent debate to verify label correctness, yielding a high-fidelity training corpus. Experiments across diverse custom policies demonstrate that small language models finetuned on our synthetic data consistently outperform state-of-the-art proprietary LLMs (including reasoning models) and dedicated guardrail models. Ablation studies confirm that both dimension decomposition and debate-based verification are critical for ensuring the diversity and label fidelity required for effective fine-tuning. The BARRED framework eliminates the reliance on extensive human annotation, offering a scalable solution for accurate custom guardrails.

AI.tldr: The paper presents BARRED, a framework for generating synthetic training data for custom policies efficiently without relying on extensive human annotation.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome challenges in developing accurate and efficient guardrails for custom policies, as existing methods like generic safety models and prompting LLMs have significant limitations, and custom classifiers require large labeled datasets.
AI.method: BARRED uses task descriptions and unlabeled examples to create synthetic data. It decomposes the domain into dimensions for coverage and employs multi-agent debate for label verification.
AI.result: Experiments show that small language models finetuned on BARRED's synthetic data outperform state-of-the-art models, demonstrating the framework's robustness and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: BARRED provides a scalable solution for training custom classifiers by eliminating the need for extensive manual labeling, enhancing performance in diverse policy contexts.

title: Towards Seamless Lunar Mosaics: Deep Radiometric Normalization for Cross-Sensor Orbital Imagery Using Chandrayaan-2 TMC Data
summary:
Radiometric inconsistencies remain a major challenge in generating seamless lunar mosaics from multi-mission orbital imagery due to variability in illumination geometry, sensor characteristics, and acquisition conditions. This paper presents a deep learning-based radiometric normalization framework for multi-mission lunar mosaics constructed primarily from ISRO's Chandrayaan-2 Terrain Mapping Camera (TMC) data, supplemented with auxiliary imagery from the SELENE (Kaguya) mission.
  The proposed approach employs a conditional generative adversarial network (cGAN) comprising a U-Net-based generator and a PatchGAN discriminator to learn a nonlinear radiometric mapping from conventionally mosaicked lunar imagery to a photometrically consistent reference derived from LROC Wide Angle Camera (WAC) data. A patch-based training strategy with overlap-aware inference is adopted to enable scalable processing of large-area mosaics while preserving structural continuity across tile boundaries.
  Quantitative evaluation using Structural Similarity Index (SSIM), Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), and Root Mean Square Error (RMSE) demonstrates consistent improvements over traditional histogram-based normalization techniques. The proposed framework achieves enhanced tonal uniformity, reduced seam artifacts, and improved structural coherence across multi-source lunar datasets.
  These results highlight the effectiveness of learning-based radiometric normalization for large-scale planetary mosaicking and demonstrate its potential for generating high-fidelity lunar surface maps from heterogeneous orbital imagery.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a deep learning framework for radiometric normalization in lunar mosaics, addressing challenges from multi-mission imagery inconsistencies.
AI.motivation: To tackle radiometric inconsistencies caused by illumination, sensor disparities, and acquisition conditions in multi-mission lunar imagery.
AI.method: A conditional GAN (cGAN) framework with a U-Net generator and PatchGAN discriminator is used to map lunar imagery to a photometrically consistent reference, employing a patch-based strategy for scalable processing.
AI.result: Improves tonal uniformity, reduces seam artifacts, and enhances structural coherence, outperforming traditional histogram-based normalization techniques as evaluated using SSIM, PSNR, and RMSE metrics.
AI.conclusion: The method effectively addresses radiometric inconsistencies in lunar mosaics and shows promise for high-fidelity planetary mapping from heterogeneous datasets.

title: Nemotron 3 Nano Omni: Efficient and Open Multimodal Intelligence
summary: We introduce Nemotron 3 Nano Omni, the latest model in the Nemotron multimodal series and the first to natively support audio inputs alongside text, images, and video. Nemotron 3 Nano Omni delivers consistent accuracy improvements over its predecessor, Nemotron Nano V2 VL, across all modalities, enabled by advances in architecture, training data and recipes. In particular, Nemotron 3 delivers leading results in real-world document understanding, long audio-video comprehension, and agentic computer use. Built on the highly efficient Nemotron 3 Nano 30B-A3B backbone, Nemotron 3 Nano Omni further incorporates innovative multimodal token-reduction techniques to deliver substantially lower inference latency and higher throughput than other models of similar size. We are releasing model checkpoints in BF16, FP8, and FP4 formats, along with portions of the training data and codebase to facilitate further research and development.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Nemotron 3 Nano Omni, a new multimodal model supporting audio, text, images, and video inputs with superior performance and efficiency.
AI.motivation: To advance multimodal AI by improving accuracy and efficiency across multiple data modalities, including audio inputs.
AI.method: Use of Nemotron 3 Nano 30B-A3B backbone with advanced multimodal token-reduction techniques, improved architecture, training data, and recipes.
AI.result: Achieved leading results in document understanding, audio-video comprehension, and efficient performance with reduced latency.
AI.conclusion: Nemotron 3 Nano Omni sets a new benchmark in multimodal AI and is released for further research with checkpoints, training data, and code.

title: KinDER: A Physical Reasoning Benchmark for Robot Learning and Planning
comment: Project website: https://prpl-group.com/kinder-site/. 21 pages, 8 figures. Accepted to Robotics Science and Systems (RSS), 2026
summary: Robotic systems that interact with the physical world must reason about kinematic and dynamic constraints imposed by their own embodiment, their environment, and the task at hand. We introduce KinDER, a benchmark for Kinematic and Dynamic Embodied Reasoning that targets physical reasoning challenges arising in robot learning and planning. KinDER comprises 25 procedurally generated environments, a Gymnasium-compatible Python library with parameterized skills and demonstrations, and a standardized evaluation suite with 13 implemented baselines spanning task and motion planning, imitation learning, reinforcement learning, and foundation-model-based approaches. The environments are designed to isolate five core physical reasoning challenges: basic spatial relations, nonprehensile multi-object manipulation, tool use, combinatorial geometric constraints, and dynamic constraints, disentangled from perception, language understanding, and application-specific complexity. Empirical evaluation shows that existing methods struggle to solve many of the environments, indicating substantial gaps in current approaches to physical reasoning. We additionally include real-to-sim-to-real experiments on a mobile manipulator to assess the correspondence between simulation and real-world physical interaction. KinDER is fully open-sourced and intended to enable systematic comparison across diverse paradigms for advancing physical reasoning in robotics. Website and code: https://prpl-group.com/kinder-site/

AI.tldr: The paper introduces KinDER, a benchmark for assessing robotic systems' physical reasoning in a range of procedurally generated environments emphasizing kinematics and dynamics.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges in robotic systems' physical reasoning essential for effective interaction in real-world tasks, where current methods show significant shortcomings.
AI.method: The authors designed KinDER with 25 procedural environments, a Python library, parameterized skills, and 13 baseline algorithms focusing on kinematics and dynamics, separated from perception and language factors.
AI.result: Experiments show that existing methods struggle across many tasks in KinDER environments, revealing gaps in current physical reasoning capabilities.
AI.conclusion: KinDER offers a systematic, open-source framework for benchmarking and improving physical reasoning in robotics, bridging the gap between simulation and real-world application.

title: From Threads to Trajectories: A Multi-LLM Pipeline for Community Knowledge Extraction from GitHub Issue Discussions
summary: Resolution of complex post-production issues in large-scale open-source software (OSS) projects requires significant cognitive effort, as developers need to go through long, unstructured and fragmented issue discussion threads before that. In this paper, we present SWE-MIMIC-Bench, an issue trajectory dataset generated from raw GitHub discussions using an automated multi-LLM pipeline. Unlike simple summarization, this pipeline utilizes a group of closed-source LLMs to perform granular tasks: analyzing individual comments with awareness of externally-linked resources, classifying comment analyses into label-specific fields (e.g., root cause, solution plan, implementation progress), and synthesizing label-aware trajectories which capture a structured and coherent narrative of the entire discussion thread. Our pipeline uses five closed-source LLM configurations for distinct purposes: label classification, inline code block and external link summarization, comment analysis, label-specific field classification and trajectory synthesis. By generating concise and reliable trajectories from complex conversation threads, this system can assist developers and researchers of broader software engineering community to understand the experience-driven collaborative approach for issue diagnosis. Furthermore, the generated trajectories can be used to train modern LLM agents to think and act like an expert developer. We evaluated our system on 800 real-world GitHub issues drawn from the SWE-Bench-Pro, SWE-Bench-Multilingual and SWE-Bench-Verified dataset, achieving a 91.7% success rate in extracting 734 high-fidelity reasoning trajectories.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SWE-MIMIC-Bench, a dataset for structuring GitHub issue discussions using a multi-LLM automated pipeline. It converts raw discussions into coherent narratives, aiding developers in solving complex software issues.
AI.motivation: Developers face challenges in resolving complex software production issues due to unstructured and fragmented discussion threads on GitHub. A structured and concise narrative of these discussions would simplify the resolution and understanding process.
AI.method: The authors implemented an automated multi-LLM pipeline using five configurations of closed-source LLMs to analyze, classify, summarize, and synthesize GitHub issue discussions into structured trajectories.
AI.result: The system achieved a 91.7% success rate in producing 734 coherent and high-fidelity trajectories from 800 GitHub issues across three datasets.
AI.conclusion: SWE-MIMIC-Bench provides developers and researchers with an effective tool to understand GitHub discussions and could also serve as a dataset to train LLM agents for expert developer-like reasoning and decision-making.

title: SciEval: A Benchmark for Automatic Evaluation of K-12 Science Instructional Materials
summary: The need to evaluate instructional materials for K-12 science education has become increasingly important, as more educators use generative AI to create instructional materials. However, the review of instructional materials is time-consuming, expertise-intensive, and difficult to scale, motivating interest in automated evaluation approaches. While large language models (LLMs) have shown strong performance on general evaluation tasks, their performance and reliability on instructional materials remain unclear. To address this gap, we formulate Automatic Instructional Materials Evaluation (AIME) as a generative AI task that predicts scores and evidence using the rubric designed by the educator. We create a benchmark dataset and develop baseline models for AIME. First, we curate the first AIME dataset, SciEval, consisting of instructional materials annotated with pedagogy-aligned evaluation scores and evidence-based rationales. Expert annotations achieve high inter-rater reliability, resulting in a dataset of 273 lesson-level instructional materials evaluated across 13 criteria (N=3549) using the EQuIP rubric. Second, we test mainstream LLMs (GPT, Gemini, Llama, and Qwen) on SciEval and find that none achieve strong performance. Then we fine-tune Qwen3 on SciEval. Results on a held-out test set show that domain-aligned fine-tuning can achieve up to 11 percent performance gains, highlighting the importance of domain-specific fine-tuning for AIME and facilitating the use of LLMs in other educational tasks.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Automatic Instructional Materials Evaluation (AIME) to automate the review of K-12 science materials, creates the SciEval dataset, and improves evaluation with domain-specific fine-tuning of LLMs.
AI.motivation: The growing use of generative AI for creating K-12 science educational materials necessitates efficient evaluation tools, as manual reviews are laborious and resource-intensive.
AI.method: The study formulates AIME as an AI-driven task, compiles the SciEval dataset annotated with pedagogy-aligned scores and rationales, and fine-tunes Qwen3 on this dataset for improvements.
AI.result: Mainstream LLMs underperform on the SciEval benchmark. Fine-tuning Qwen3 on SciEval achieves up to 11% performance improvement, emphasizing the value of domain-specific approaches.
AI.conclusion: Domain-specific fine-tuning of LLMs enhances their utility for evaluating educational materials, paving the way for broader adoption of AI in instructional review tasks.

title: Below-Chance Blindness: Prompted Underperformance in Small LLMs Produces Positional Bias Rather than Answer Avoidance
summary: Detecting sandbagging--the deliberate underperformance on capability evaluations--is an open problem in AI safety. We tested whether symptom validity testing (SVT) logic from clinical malingering detection could identify sandbagging through below-chance performance (BCB) on forced-choice items. In a pre-registered pilot at the 7-9 billion parameter instruction-tuned scale (3 models, 4 MMLU-Pro domains, 4 conditions, 500 items per cell, 24,000 total trials), the plausibility gate failed. Zero of 12 model-domain cells showed significant below-chance performance under sandbagging instruction. Exploratory analyses revealed three qualitatively distinct failure modes. Qwen-2.5-7B and Phi-3.5-mini largely ignored the sandbagging instruction, with 62-88% response identity with the honest baseline. Llama-3-8B complied substantially but implemented underperformance as a positional heuristic, collapsing its response distribution onto middle-alphabet options (E at 31.8%, F at 26.1%) regardless of where the correct answer fell. This produced accuracy boosts of up to 33 percentage points when the correct answer coincidentally occupied the model's preferred position. An explicit anti-task instruction ("pick the least likely answer") drove two of three models below chance, with accuracy as low as 0.024. The capability for answer-aware avoidance therefore exists but is not activated by "deliberately underperform." BCB did not fail as a logical marker of answer-aware avoidance. It was not observed in this regime because the model showing the largest behavioural shift exhibited behaviour consistent with a position-dominant response policy rather than content-aware answer avoidance. We propose that positional-distribution shift may be a more effective behavioural signature than below-chance accuracy for detecting prompted underperformance at this model scale.

AI.tldr: The paper explores the detection of deliberate underperformance (sandbagging) in AI models through clinical methods, finding positional shifts, not below-chance performance, as a better indicator.
AI.motivation: To address the safety concern of AI deliberately underperforming during capability evaluations and develop a reliable detection mechanism.
AI.method: Used a pilot test with 7-9 billion parameter instruction-tuned models across various conditions, analyzing response patterns, accuracy, and behavioural shifts.
AI.result: Models showed distinct failure modes: non-compliance, positional biases, and activation under explicit anti-task instructions, but failed below-chance performance detection in general.
AI.conclusion: The positional-distribution shift is posited as a better behavioural marker for prompted underperformance detection in current-scale models.

title: When the Forger Is the Judge: GPT-Image-2 Cannot Recognize Its Own Faked Documents
summary: OpenAI's GPT-Image-2 has effectively erased the visual boundary between authentic and AI-edited document images: a single number on a receipt can be replaced in under a second for a few cents. We release AIForge-Doc v2, a paired dataset of 3,066 GPT-Image-2 document forgeries with pixel-precise masks in DocTamper-compatible format, and benchmark four lines of defence: human inspectors (N=120, n=365 pair-votes via the public 2AFC site CanUSpotAI.com), TruFor (generic forensic), DocTamper (qcf-568, document-specific), and the same GPT-Image-2 model as a zero-shot self-judge -- asked, to avoid the trivial "image is mostly real" reading, whether any region was generated or edited by an AI image model.
